AMAT.headline: Revenue dipped -2% YoY in Q1 FY26 as trailing-edge logic weakened, but DRAM/HBM mix jumped 7pts — the AI-driven memory spend is now visible inside semi systems

shifts.trend: DRAM jumped to 34% of Semiconductor Systems mix in Q1 FY26 vs 27% in FY25 full-year, as HBM-technology-transition capex offset trailing-edge logic decline
shifts.note: Foundry/logic fell from 67% to 62% mix — China trailing-edge weakness, not structural loss

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B (-16% YoY) in FY25; Q1 FY26 China $2.1B (-7% YoY) as US export controls continue to constrain. Taiwan jumped +71% to $6.9B (FY25) and +46% YoY in Q1
shifts.note: Q1 FY26 $253M BIS export-controls compliance legal-settlement charge hit operating margin -4.3pts to 26.1%

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $28.4B (+4% YoY); Q1 FY26 $7.0B (-2% YoY) — cycle rotation, not share loss; AGS services +3%/+15% maintained both periods
shifts.note: Management guided continued strength from HBM adoption and AI compute — revenue dip is trailing-edge noise

AMAT.verdict: Thesis is transition-dependent: DRAM/HBM mix is visibly rising and offsets trailing-edge drag, but the China export settlement ($253M) and logic softness cap near-term upside. Watch the DRAM mix shift — if it reaches 40%+ of semi systems, the AI cycle overwhelms the headwinds.

AMD.headline: Data Center acceleration from +32% YoY (FY25) to +57% YoY (Q1 FY26); DC operating margin expanded from 22% to 28% as MI350 ramp absorbed the MI308 China charge noise

shifts.trend: Total revenue +34% YoY (FY25) → +38% YoY (Q1 FY26); Data Center $16.6B (+32% FY25) → $5.8B (+57% Q1 FY26, now 56% of total revenue)
shifts.note: OpenAI 6 GW multi-year deal (Oct 2025) provides backlog visibility through MI450 cycle and beyond

shifts.trend: Gross margin 50% (FY25) → 53% (+3pts Q1 FY26) on richer DC mix; DC operating margin 22% (FY25) → 28% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: FY25 DC margin was depressed by $440M net MI308 China export charges — Q1 FY26 now shows the clean underlying rate

shifts.trend: FY25 hit by $800M MI308 inventory charge (Q2) then $360M reversal (Q4); Feb 2026 MI325 China licenses granted but carry 25% US re-import tariff — partial normalization
shifts.note: China was 22% of FY25 revenue ($7.8B); tariff-on-licenses constrains economics but re-opens the market

shifts.trend: ZT Systems closed $3.2B (Mar 2025) for rack-scale AI platform (Helios); manufacturing arm sold to Sanmina $2.4B Oct 2025 — net capex recycled into design IP
shifts.note: FY26 will feature annual Instinct cadence MI350/MI355X/MI450 — sustained R&D spending at +39% YoY in Q1 FY26

AMD.verdict: Thesis accelerating: Data Center is structurally re-rating as MI350 GPU ramp + EPYC server momentum compound. The 57% Q1 DC growth vs 32% FY25 rate is not noise — the OpenAI 6 GW deal and partial China re-opening are additive. Main risk: DC operating margin (28%) remains well below NVDA's ~60%+; AMD is still buying share with lower margins.

AMKR.headline: Revenue recovery broadening — Q1 2026 all four end markets grew; capex step-change from $0.9B (FY25) to $2.5-3.0B (FY26) for Arizona advanced-packaging fab signals multi-year strategic repositioning

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M (13.5% of sales); FY26 guided $2.5-3.0B (reaffirmed Q1) driven by Arizona Facility construction (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing begins H1 2028)
shifts.note: Q1 2026 capex $224.6M vs $79.9M Q1 2025 — already 3x prior year pace; CHIPS Act $407M direct funding + 35% investment tax credit

shifts.trend: FY25 net sales $6.71B (+6.2% YoY); Q1 2026 net sales $1.68B (+27.5% YoY) with all four end markets growing — communications +42%, automotive/industrial +28%, computing +19%
shifts.note: Computing growth attributed to datacenter (read: HBM/2.5D packaging demand)

shifts.trend: Gross margin 14.0% (FY25, pressured by Vietnam ramp) → 14.2% (+2.3pts YoY Q1 2026) on higher factory utilization
shifts.note: Vietnam ramp inefficiency from FY25 fading as throughput improves

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8-30% of net sales across both periods; Qualcomm 11%; top-10 = 72% of 2025 sales
shifts.note: Customer concentration risk persists — single-customer dependency limits pricing power

AMKR.verdict: Thesis intact: the $2.5-3.0B capex step-change is the signal — Arizona advanced-packaging is a direct bet on HBM/2.5D assembly demand for AI compute. The 27.5% Q1 revenue acceleration validates the investment thesis. Main risk is the construction timeline (manufacturing starts H1 2028), leaving a two-year window of capex-heavy / limited-revenue drag.

AMZN.headline: AWS growth re-accelerated from +20% (FY25) to +28% (Q1 2026); RPO leapt from $244B to $364B on OpenAI $100B expansion; capex on a $170B+ annual run-rate by Q1 2026

shifts.trend: AWS revenue FY25 $128.7B (+20% YoY); Q1 2026 $37.6B (+28% YoY) — re-acceleration driven by AI workload ramp
shifts.note: AWS operating margin compressed slightly to 37.7% (Q1 2026) vs 39.4% prior year Q1 on infrastructure investment, down from 35.4% FY25 full-year

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $128.3B (vs $77.7B in 2024, +65%); Q1 2026 capex $43.2B vs $24.3B Q1 2025 (+78%), majority for AWS
shifts.note: FY25 guided 2026 capex to increase; Q1 pace implies $170B+ annualized — consistent with 'significantly increase' language

shifts.trend: AWS-related RPO $244B at Dec 31 2025 → $364B at Mar 31 2026 (+49% in one quarter); OpenAI expanded existing $38B AWS commitment by $100B over 8 years
shifts.note: Q1 also included $15B OpenAI Series C + $50B total commitment and $25B+ Anthropic investments — hyperscaler AI workload lock-in deepening

AMZN.verdict: AWS AI demand is re-accelerating and the RPO jump to $364B locks in multi-year revenue. The capex escalation (from $128B to an implied $170B+ annualized) is the critical demand signal for the entire compute stack — every layer from GPU to power depends on Amazon following through.

ANET.headline: Revenue acceleration from +28.6% (FY25) to +35.1% (Q1 FY26); product deferred revenue swelled from $5.37B to $6.20B in one quarter as AI Ethernet trials convert to firm backlog

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $9.0B (+28.6% YoY); Q1 FY26 $2.71B (+35.1% YoY) on AI Ethernet shipments; product revenue +36.6%
shifts.note: AI Ethernet acceptance-period contracts create revenue recognition lag — reported revenues understating true demand booking

shifts.trend: Memory and silicon supply tightening explicitly cited as gross-margin pressure in both FY25 10-K and Q1 FY26 10-Q; ANET procuring ahead of demand
shifts.note: Deferred revenue $5.37B (FY25) → $6.20B (+$826M Q1 FY26) — backlog building faster than recognition

shifts.trend: Gross margin 64.1% (FY25) → 61.9% (-2.2pts Q1 FY26) on large-customer mix and memory/silicon cost pressure
shifts.note: Management explicitly warns large customer pricing discounts continue to compress GM on big orders

shifts.trend: Americas mix rose to 84.5% of Q1 FY26 revenue (vs 79.7% prior year), reflecting US-based Cloud Titan concentration; Cloud and AI Titans 48% of FY25 revenue
shifts.note: Two end customers at 26% and 16% of FY25 revenue — hyperscaler dependency increasing

ANET.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening: the deferred revenue build ($6.20B, +$826M in one quarter) is the most bullish forward signal — AI Ethernet is converting from trial to committed deployment. The gross-margin compression is the price of winning large-scale AI fabric deals; monitor whether it stabilizes as Etherlink portfolio matures.

AVGO.headline: Semiconductor solutions re-accelerated from +22% (FY25) to +52% (Q1 FY26) as XPU hyperscaler pull-through intensifies; top-5 customer concentration jumped from ~40% to ~50% of revenue in one year

shifts.trend: Semiconductor solutions FY25 $36.9B (+22%); Q1 FY26 $12.5B (+52% YoY) on custom AI accelerators (XPUs) and AI networking; total revenue $63.9B → $19.3B Q1 at +29%
shifts.note: Semi solutions rose to 65% of Q1 FY26 revenue (vs 55% prior-year Q1) — AI semi mix is re-weighting the company

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers ~40% of FY25 revenue → ~50% of Q1 FY26 revenue; one distributor (XPU hyperscaler channel) = 32% of FY25 → 42% of Q1 FY26
shifts.note: FY25 warned AI rack/system leasing model will compress gross margin — first explicit framing of XPU rack-sale as margin-dilutive

shifts.trend: Gross margin 68% (FY25) → 68% (Q1 FY26) — held flat despite higher semi mix; operating margin improved 40% → 44%
shifts.note: VMware fully absorbed, restructuring tail essentially gone; software mix (infrastructure software $6.8B, +1%) dampens potential semi margin upside

shifts.trend: China shipments 20% of FY24 revenue → 17% of FY25 revenue; US export restrictions on certain Chinese customers expected to continue
shifts.note: FY25 10-K disclosed: Chinese end-customer exposure substantially lower than shipment destination figure

AVGO.verdict: Thesis is the dominant XPU cycle play: semi revenue +52% YoY in Q1 FY26 with stable 68% gross margin is an exceptional combination. The concentration risk (one distributor = 42% of revenue) is the key thesis-break watchpoint — any hyperscaler pause or platform-switch risk is asymmetric given that dependency.

CAT.headline: Backlog jumped from $30.0B (YE24) to $51.2B (YE25) and keeps growing; Power Generation +32% YoY in FY25 and +22% YoY in Q1 FY26 as prime power demand from data centers trends higher

shifts.trend: Power & Energy sales +12% to $32.2B (FY25); Power Generation sub-segment $10.3B (+32%); Q1 FY26 P&E revenues $7.0B (+22% YoY) with Power Generation continuing to grow
shifts.note: Data center prime power orders explicitly trending higher — first mentioned FY25, reaffirmed Q1 FY26

shifts.trend: Order backlog $30.0B (YE24) → $51.2B (YE25) → robust continued growth in Q1 FY26; $19.3B of YE25 backlog not expected to fill in 2026
shifts.note: Backlog growth driven primarily by Power & Energy segment — data-center-driven prime power lead times extending

shifts.trend: P&E profit margin 19.9% (FY25, flat YoY); adjusted operating margin 18.0% (Q1 FY26 vs 18.3%) — modest compression from tariff manufacturing costs offset by volume
shifts.note: Q2 2026 tariff costs expected ~$700M; FY26 tariff headwind $2.2-2.4B flagged as primary margin risk

shifts.trend: FY26 guidance low double-digit revenue growth (top end of 5-7% CAGR target) maintained in both filings; capex ~$3.5B consistent
shifts.note: SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs in Feb 2026 but $1.0B already incurred; recovery not probable — guidance held despite tariff uncertainty

CAT.verdict: Power generation demand from data centers is the clear incremental driver — backlog more than doubled in one year and continues building. Thesis intact: CAT's heavy-duty reciprocating engines for prime power are supply-constrained. Tariff cost pressure ($2.2-2.4B FY26) is the primary near-term risk to margin, but top-line growth is structural.

CDNS.headline: Revenue accelerated from +14% YoY (FY25) to +19% YoY (Q1 FY26); backlog grew to $8.0B from $7.8B; China revenue jumped +36% YoY in Q1 FY26 despite BIS whiplash

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5.30B (+14% YoY); Q1 FY26 $1.47B (+19% YoY); broad-based growth with Other Americas +87%, Japan +36%, Other Asia +23%
shifts.note: Recurring revenue 82% (FY25) → 77% (Q1 FY26) as hardware/up-front mix grew — near-term margin trade-off

shifts.trend: China revenue FY25 $680M (+19%, 13% of mix); Q1 FY26 China $189.4M (+36% YoY, 13% of mix) despite Sep 2025 BIS 50%-owned-affiliate rule and prior May/Jul 2025 is-informed whiplash
shifts.note: Company expects 'limited' impact from current trade controls — July 2025 rescission held; China is outperforming EDA peer Synopsys

shifts.trend: RPO backlog $7.8B (YE25) → $8.0B (Q1 FY26); 53% → 55% expected in next 12 months — near-term coverage improving
shifts.note: No supply constraints for EDA — this is a demand/bookings metric

shifts.trend: May 2025 BIS 'is-informed' EDA export license requirement imposed, then rescinded July 2, 2025; Q1 FY26 sees 'limited' expansion impact — regulatory risk materially reduced vs H1 FY25
shifts.note: Hexagon D&E acquisition closed Q1 FY26 (revolving credit drawn) — inorganic growth now contributing

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact: EDA is a structural gating input to every advanced node and advanced-packaging ramp, with recurring revenue >77% and backlog growing. China outperformance versus peers and July 2025 BIS rescission are positive differentiators. Monitor whether the hardware/upfront revenue mix shift durably compresses margins.

CEG.headline: Calpine closed Jan 7, 2026 — Constellation is now the world's largest private power producer at 55 GW; nuclear PPA pipeline with tech companies materialized from LOI to closed deal (Meta, Microsoft)

shifts.trend: Standalone CEG: ~33 GW nuclear; Post-Calpine: 55 GW combined (21 GW gas, 730 MW geothermal, 800 MW battery + 2.5M retail accounts)
shifts.note: Calpine adds 60 TWh retail load including 75% of Fortune 100 — diversification beyond nuclear

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: Crane Clean Energy Center (TMI Unit 1, 835 MW, Microsoft 20-yr PPA) received $1.0B DOE loan guarantee Nov 2025; FY25 closing capex significantly larger post-Calpine $22B deal
shifts.note: NY PSC approved 20-year ZEC extension Jan 2026 — state subsidy secured for nuclear fleet through 2049

shifts.trend: FY25 operating revenues $25.5B vs $23.6B (+8%); adjusted EPS $9.39 vs $8.67; tech PPA pipeline grew from June 2025 (Meta Clinton 20-yr PPA) to Nov 2025 DOE loan guarantee and FY25 full close
shifts.note: GAAP net income declined ($2.32B vs $3.75B) on lower nuclear PTC — operating cash flows normalize with Calpine contribution

CEG.verdict: Thesis transformed by Calpine: CEG went from nuclear-only to the dominant US baseload provider in one deal. The Microsoft and Meta long-term PPAs (20+ years) provide visible contracted revenue from AI demand. Regulatory milestones (ZEC extensions, DOE loan guarantee) de-risk the nuclear fleet. The main new risk is integration complexity and $22B of Calpine acquisition leverage.

CLS.headline: Revenue re-accelerated from +42% CCS (FY25) to +76% CCS (Q1 FY26); HPS networking switch revenue maintained +63-81% trajectory as hyperscaler switch programs continue ramping

shifts.trend: FY25 total revenue $12.2B with CCS +42% to $9.19B, HPS +81% to $5.0B; Q1 FY26 revenue $4.05B (+53% total), CCS +76% to $3.24B, HPS +63% to ~$1.7B
shifts.note: Enterprise end market +101% in Q1 FY26 on next-gen AI/ML compute program ramp at the hyperscaler customer that paused in 2025 — full recovery confirmed

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $1B (~6% of revenue, vs historical 1.5-2.0%); Q1 FY26 expansions in Thailand, US, Mexico, Japan; revolver upsized from $750M to $1.75B in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: New $250M Term A Loan refinanced and extended to April 2031 — balance sheet structured for multi-year capacity scale-up

shifts.trend: CCS segment margin 8.0% (Q1 FY25) → 8.4% (Q4 FY25) → 8.6% (Q1 FY26); adjusted EPS $2.16 beat high end of $1.95-2.15 guide
shifts.note: Operating leverage from HPS scale-up offsetting capacity investment — margin expansion trajectory confirmed across two quarters

shifts.trend: FY25: 3 CCS customers each >10% (32%, 14%, 12%), top 10 = 79% of revenue; Q1 FY26: HPS 42% of total revenue (up from 41%)
shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependency is the business model — revolver upsizing reflects confidence in contract durability

CLS.verdict: Thesis remains fully intact: HPS networking switch ramps are sustained, margin is expanding, and the revolver upsizing signals management confidence in the scale-up. The Enterprise recovery (+101%) eliminates the FY25 air pocket risk. Main watch: 32% single-customer concentration and 79% top-10 exposure mean a hyperscaler pause would materially impact results.

CMI.headline: Power Systems EBITDA margin expanded from 22.7% (FY25) to 29.5% (Q1 FY26) — data-center-driven power generation demand is delivering structural margin improvement, not just volume uplift

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA margin 18.4% (FY24) → 22.7% (FY25) → 29.5% (Q1 FY26); EBITDA +44% YoY (FY25) and +48% YoY (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Operational leverage on data-center demand plus favorable pricing driving the step-function — not a one-quarter anomaly

shifts.trend: Power Systems FY25 sales $7.46B (+16%); Q1 FY26 $1.96B (+19% YoY); Power Generation FY25 $4.73B (+19%); Q1 FY26 $1.28B (+28% YoY)
shifts.note: Data center backlog stretches 6-8 quarters (FY25 10-K); international +16% in Q1 FY26 on China and Europe power generation strength

shifts.trend: Both filings maintain 'strong demand for data center products extending 6-8 quarters out'; capacity ramp ongoing; Power Systems strong demand outlook consistently reiterated
shifts.note: FY25 consolidated revenue -1% YoY on absence of $1.3B Atmus gain + lower truck demand — Power Systems is the bright spot in a mixed portfolio

CMI.verdict: Data-center power generation is a structural high-margin growth driver for Cummins — margin expansion to 29.5% EBITDA in Q1 FY26 confirms pricing power. The 6-8 quarter visibility window is exceptional for industrials. Risk: truck end-market weakness (-6% US/Canada Q1 FY26) offsets at the consolidated level; watch whether the 6-8 quarter DC backlog holds through 2026-2027 when supply additions come online.

COHR.headline: D&C segment accelerated from +34% to +41% YoY over two quarters; gross margin expanded 140bps → 243bps as AI datacenter transceiver demand compounds with 'scale across' as a new demand vector

shifts.trend: D&C revenue +34% to $1.21B (Q2 FY26) → +41% to $1.36B (Q3 FY26); total revenue +17% → +21% YoY; 9-month D&C $3.66B (+34%)
shifts.note: Q3 introduced 'scale across' interconnect demand as a named driver alongside DCI and telecom — AI scale-across optics is a new category emerging in 2026

shifts.trend: Gross margin +140bps YoY (Q2 FY26) → +243bps YoY (Q3 FY26) on D&C yield improvements and pricing optimization in transceivers
shifts.note: Consolidated gross margin 37% both periods; D&C segment profit +44% → +49% YoY as mix shift to high-margin transceiver products compounds

shifts.trend: Portfolio narrowing: aerospace/defense divested Sep 2, 2025; Munich Germany business divested Jan 30, 2026 — COHR reshaping toward AI/data-center optics
shifts.note: Industrial segment -10% (Q2) → -16% (Q3) on consecutive divestitures; D&C now the pure signal

COHR.verdict: Thesis strengthening: the acceleration from +34% to +41% D&C growth and 243bps gross-margin expansion confirm the AI transceiver cycle is compounding. The new 'scale across' demand category (inter-datacenter AI fabric) is additive. Portfolio divestitures are value-creating — as Industrial shrinks, the margin profile clarifies upward.

CRM.headline: RPO recovered from $59.5B → $72.4B; cRPO +16% signals near-term acceleration; Informatica $9.6B acquisition expands the agentic data management layer

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 revenue $10.26B (+9% YoY); FY26 full-year $41.5B (+10%); cRPO +16% outpacing total RPO +14% — near-term demand accelerating
shifts.note: Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack/Other grew 19% YoY (Q3 FY26) as the AI/consumption bucket — the only double-digit segment

shifts.trend: RPO $59.5B (Oct 2025, below YE24 $63.4B) → $72.4B (Jan 2026, +14% YoY) — contract signings recovered strongly in Q4 FY26
shifts.note: Q3 RPO decline (from $63.4B to $59.5B) was a concern; FY26 full-year reversal to $72.4B shows the renewal/expansion cycle intact

shifts.trend: Repurchased $12.7B of stock (50M shares) + $1.6B dividends in FY26; operating cash flow $15.0B (+15%)
shifts.note: Closing Informatica at $9.6B did not prevent record capital returns — FCF conversion is high

CRM.verdict: Thesis intact: the RPO recovery to $72.4B and cRPO +16% growth confirm Agentforce demand is translating into contracted revenue. Consumption-pricing uncertainty (mgmt flagged 'relatively new and uncertain' Agentforce/Data Cloud markets in Q3) is the key risk — if consumption underdelivers against contracted commitments, revenue recognition lags. Operating cash flow strength provides a buffer.

CRWV.headline: Revenue +168% (FY25) then +112% (Q1 2026); RPO exploded from $15.1B (YE24) to $60.7B (YE25) to $98.8B (Q1 2026) — but accumulated deficit $3.4B and debt $21.6B at 9-15% average rate

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5.1B (+168% YoY); Q1 2026 $2.08B (+112% YoY) — growth still enormous but decelerating from a higher base
shifts.note: RPO $60.7B (YE25) → $98.8B (Q1 2026) suggests future revenue is structurally committed; 36% recognized in first 24 months

shifts.trend: Microsoft (Customer A) = 67% of FY25 revenue → 45% of Q1 2026 revenue (vs 72% Q1 2025); Meta emerged as 20% customer in Q1 2026
shifts.note: Diversification from 77% Microsoft (2024 top-2) to 45% shows customer base broadening — but still a 2-customer story

shifts.trend: FY25 total debt $21.6B (vs $8.0B prior year) at 9-15% effective rates; Q1 2026 net loss $740M vs $315M; accumulated deficit $3.4B; post-quarter raised additional $7.8B ($4.0B 1.75% converts + $2.8B 9.75% + $1.0B equity)
shifts.note: $107M net income sensitivity per 100bps — highly leveraged to interest rates; 850 MW active out of 3.1 GW contracted = significant future capex required

shifts.trend: Capex $10.3B (FY25); 850 MW active (vs 360 MW prior year) across 43 data centers; 3.1 GW total contracted power — requires ~$35B+ further capex to fill contracted capacity
shifts.note: Committed $1.2B for two new data center JV equity stakes in Q1 2026

CRWV.verdict: The RPO-to-revenue model is the bull case: $98.8B RPO at 36% conversion in 24 months implies $35B+ of contractually committed revenue. But the debt stack ($21.6B at 9-15%, growing) is the structural risk — every GW of capacity addition deepens the leverage. Microsoft concentration easing (67% → 45%) is positive diversification. Net: thesis viable but execution-dependent; debt service coverage is the fragility point.

CSCO.headline: Networking accelerated from +15% (Q1 FY26) to +21% (Q2 FY26) YoY as AI Infrastructure orders continue converting; Security declined in both quarters on Splunk transition drag

shifts.trend: Total revenue Q1 +8% → Q2 +10% YoY; Networking product category Q1 +15% (+$1.0B) → Q2 +21% (+$1.4B); 1H FY26 Networking +18% (+$2.5B) led by AI Infrastructure and Data Center Switching
shifts.note: Service Provider and Cloud customer market led product growth in both quarters across Americas and EMEA

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26: inventory +7% and purchase commitments +10% vs FYE 2025, explicitly tied to Cisco Silicon One manufacturing capacity for hyperscalers; same buildup continuing Q2
shifts.note: Silicon One commitments raise supply-chain obsolescence risk — CSCO building inventory ahead of demand

shifts.trend: Security revenue -4% (Q2 FY26) on Splunk shift from on-prem to cloud subscriptions and prior-gen product declines; trend expected to continue 2H FY26
shifts.note: Security drag partially masks overall strength — Networking is the AI-driven segment, Security is a headwind until Splunk cloud transition completes

CSCO.verdict: AI Infrastructure networking demand is the compelling story — 21% YoY growth in Q2 FY26 confirms the buildout is converting to orders. The Silicon One purchase-commitment buildup is a positive supply indicator. Security decline is a known headwind but transient — the Splunk cloud transition timing is the wildcard for 2H FY26 estimates.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas organic orders +42% in Q1 FY26; backlog surged from $13.2B (YE25) to $14.5B (+44% YoY) — data center is the primary growth driver across both Electrical segments

shifts.trend: Total backlog $19.8B (YE25); Electrical Americas backlog +31% YoY (YE25) → +44% YoY (Q1 FY26, $14.5B); Electrical Global backlog +19% (YE25) → +73% YoY (Q1 FY26, $3.16B)
shifts.note: Book-to-bill 1.2x Electrical Americas; organic orders +42% — lead-time extension confirming supply tightness for data-center electrical gear

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas FY25 +16%; Q1 FY26 +20% YoY ($3.60B); Electrical Global FY25 +9%; Q1 FY26 +21% YoY ($1.95B)
shifts.note: $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition (announced FY25, closing Q2 FY26) adds liquid cooling from chip to grid — extends addressable market into AI cooling

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas operating margin 29.9% (FY25) → 25.6% (Q1 FY26, -480bps); commodity and wage inflation accelerating as data center buildout pulls raw materials
shifts.note: 210bps volume leverage partially offset the 480bps commodity headwind — margin compression is a headwind but not a demand signal

ETN.verdict: Thesis intact: the backlog/order signals are unambiguous — Electrical Americas organic orders +42% and backlog +44% are best-in-class infrastructure demand readings. The Boyd Thermal liquid cooling acquisition meaningfully extends the addressable market. Near-term margin compression from commodity inflation is the main headwind; watch whether pricing power catches up to cost in 2H 2026.

FN.headline: Revenue acceleration from +35.9% (FQ2) to +39.3% (FQ3) YoY; DCI surged +90.4% and is now the fastest-growing optical line as AI scale-across interconnect demand ramps into 2026

shifts.trend: FQ2 revenue $1.13B (+35.9% YoY); FQ3 $1.21B (+39.3% YoY); 9-month $3.33B (+32.5%); DCI $142.2M (FQ2) → $196.9M (+90.4% YoY, FQ3) — fastest growing optical product
shifts.note: Telecom $412.2M → $431.4M (+42.5%) maintaining strong growth; Datacom flat/down sequentially as speed mix shifts to DCI/higher-speed

shifts.trend: Non-optical (HPC + auto) +61.1% in FQ2, HPC $85.6M first disclosure; FQ3 Non-optical +51.7%, HPC $106.7M — AI-adjacent HPC manufacturing emerging as a second growth vector
shifts.note: 9-month HPC $207.7M — run-rate now ~$280M annualized, likely growing

shifts.trend: FQ2: 4 customers each >10% (combined 59.1%) vs 2 customers (48.5%) prior year — first time at 4-customer concentration; FQ3 same 4-customer structure persists
shifts.note: Nokia/Infinera + Lumentum/NeoPhotonics + Cisco/Acacia industry consolidation flagged as customer-list risk

shifts.trend: Both filings reference Chonburi (~$133M, 2.0M sq ft) new facility construction plus customer-specific asset additions; operating cash flow Q2 $148.8M lower YoY on inventory build for higher demand
shifts.note: Inventory build confirms management confidence in near-term demand — not a warning signal

FN.verdict: Thesis is accelerating: DCI +90% is the standout data point — AI scale-across interconnect is converting from hyperscaler trial to production ramp, and Fabrinet is the pure-play contract manufacturer capturing it. The HPC non-optical line ($207.7M 9-month) is an underfollowed add-on. The Chonburi expansion is necessary for the next revenue leg. Customer concentration from industry M&A is the main risk.

GEV.headline: HA-Turbine orders 43 (FY25, vs 25 prior year) and gas turbine GW orders +47% YoY; RPO surged from $150.2B to $163.3B in one quarter; Power segment margin expanded to 16.3% from 11.6%

shifts.trend: Heavy-Duty Gas Turbine orders: 41 (2023) → 68 (2024) → 110 (2025); GW orders 20.2 (2024) → 29.8 (2025); Q1 2026: 28 heavy-duty orders (vs 29), 8.1 GW (vs 7.1) — order pace holding at elevated level
shifts.note: Total RPO $150.2B (YE25) → $163.3B (Q1 2026 end), +$39.8B YoY (+32%) — turbine delivery lead times extending

shifts.trend: Power segment EBITDA margin 12.5% (FY24) → 14.7% (FY25) → 16.3% (Q1 2026); favorable pricing and higher volume driving step-function improvement
shifts.note: FY25 net income $4.9B (+$3.3B YoY); Q1 2026 net income $4.7B (includes Prolec GE acquisition gain)

shifts.trend: Oct 2025: $5.3B Prolec GE acquisition (10,000-employee transformer JV) closed Feb 2026; Electrification RPO benefiting from Prolec + HVDC/switchgear demand
shifts.note: First commercial SMR contract in North America via Hitachi JV; SMR discussions with US Administration add optionality

shifts.trend: Gas Power: 'essential role in energy transition, reliable and dispatchable power for hyperscalers and data centers' language consistent across both filings
shifts.note: Tariff impact $250M (2025 after contractual protections); upgraded to BBB+ Fitch, BBB S&P — financing costs improving

GEV.verdict: Gas turbine orders are compounding at a pace not seen in decades — 110 heavy-duty units in FY25 (vs 41 in 2023) with a $163B RPO backstop. Data center prime power is an explicit demand driver. Prolec GE strengthens the transformer/electrification end of the power chain. Thesis intact: GEV is the single best play on grid-connected dispatchable power for AI datacenter buildout.

GNRC.headline: C&I segment accelerated from +4.9% (FY25) to +28% (Q1 FY26) on data center demand; adjusted EBITDA margin improved 4.4pts as residential drag stabilizes and C&I margin expands

shifts.trend: FY25 total sales -2% ($4.21B); C&I +4.9%; Q1 FY26 total +12% ($1.06B); C&I +28% ($510M) driven by data center customers, industrial channels, and controls solutions
shifts.note: Residential recovery (+1% Q1 vs -6.8% FY25) removes the primary drag; large-megawatt diesel gensets for data centers a new growth line

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted EBITDA margin 17.0% (vs 18.4% prior year); Q1 FY26 adjusted EBITDA $193M (+29%), residential margin 25.1% (vs 20.3%), C&I margin 13.0% (vs 11.4%)
shifts.note: Operational efficiencies in Residential and Allmand acquisition accretion driving C&I margin improvement

shifts.trend: March 2026: reorganized from Domestic/International to Residential/C&I to align with strategy; acquired Enercon Engineering for custom power equipment for hyperscale/enterprise data centers
shifts.note: FY25 10-K: large-megawatt backup generator market 'significantly supply-constrained' — first-mover positioning in data center backup power

GNRC.verdict: Thesis: data center backup power is the C&I growth driver and it's showing up — C&I +28% in Q1 FY26 with margin expansion. The Enercon acquisition (custom power for hyperscale) deepens the data center positioning. The segment reorganization to Residential/C&I makes the data center story easier to track. Main risk: residential recovery is weather-dependent; a quiet storm season returns headwinds.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud re-accelerated from +36% (FY25) to +63% (Q1 2026); RPO leapt from $242.8B to $467.6B in one quarter on cloud contract signings and TPU hardware agreements

shifts.trend: Google Cloud FY25 $58.7B (+36% YoY); Q1 2026 $20.0B (+63% YoY) — significant re-acceleration; Cloud op income $13.9B FY25 → $6.6B Q1 alone (33% margin, 3x YoY)
shifts.note: First-time disclosure of 'multi-gigawatt' TPU hardware supply agreements; revenue starts late 2026, majority 2027 — new infrastructure-as-a-product business model

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $91.4B (+74% YoY); Q1 2026 capex $35.7B; FY25 stated 'significantly increase' relative to 2025 — Q1 annualized would imply $140B+
shifts.note: Issued $31.1B senior unsecured notes Q1 2026 alone; $37.3B issued in 2025 — debt-financed capex at scale

shifts.trend: Revenue backlog $242.8B (YE25) → $467.6B (Q1 2026, +93% in one quarter); $462.3B is Google Cloud
shifts.note: Committed $40B to a private company (Mar 2026); $16B funded into Waymo Feb 2026; $29.5B Wiz + $5.9B Intersect closed — capital deployment accelerating

GOOGL.verdict: Google Cloud is now accelerating faster than AWS (+28%) and Azure (+40%) in revenue growth — +63% Q1 2026 is the standout hyperscaler print. The TPU hardware supply agreement model (multi-GW, customer-deployed) is a structural shift in how Google monetizes AI compute. The RPO jump to $467.6B in one quarter confirms the demand is contracted. Thesis: Google Cloud is the most underpriced hyperscaler AI infrastructure story.

HPE.headline: Networking +151.5% in Q1 FY26 on full-quarter Juniper; AI server growing but margin-dilutive; non-GAAP operating margin expanded to 12.7% from 9.8% on Juniper software mix

shifts.trend: FY25 Networking +51.1% (+$2.3B, $1.4B from Juniper post-July 2, 2025 close); Q1 FY26 Networking +151.5% to $2.7B as Juniper contributed first full quarter
shifts.note: Segment realignment: Server + Hybrid Cloud merged into 'Cloud & AI' — AI servers growing but carry limited margins on GPU pass-through pricing

shifts.trend: Gross margin 35.9% (+670bps YoY, Q1 FY26) on Juniper software mix; non-GAAP operating margin 12.7% (+280bps) vs FY25 9.8%
shifts.note: FY25 GAAP operating loss from $1.6B Hybrid Cloud goodwill impairment; Q1 FY26 clean quarter shows underlying margin recovery

shifts.trend: $600M Juniper synergy target by FY28 (requires $800M investment) consistently reaffirmed across both filings; ARR $3.15B (+63%) led by Networking aaS
shifts.note: Memory component shortages and tariff uncertainty flagged as Q1 FY26 new risks — not in FY25 10-K

HPE.verdict: Juniper integration is the decisive near-term driver — the margin expansion to 12.7% non-GAAP operating shows the software mix is already accruing. AI server growth is real but margin-neutral or dilutive. The $600M synergy target and ARR acceleration (+63%) support the long-term thesis. Main risk: any slowdown in Juniper aaS conversions delays the synergy timeline.

HUBB.headline: Electrical Solutions organic growth stable at 7-10% across both periods on consistent data center strength; Utility Solutions adding T&D growth; margins modestly pressured by tariffs but expanding adjusted

shifts.trend: FY25 Electrical Solutions +7.1% (organic +7.2%); Q1 FY26 +11.8% (organic +10.6%) — datacenter and light industrial consistently driving both periods
shifts.note: Utility Solutions FY25 +2.0% (organic +1.1%); Q1 FY26 +10% (organic +6.8%) on T&D acceleration — second growth engine emerging

shifts.trend: FY25 operating margin +130bps to 20.7%; Q1 FY26 adjusted operating margin +110bps overall; Utility Solutions Q1 margin +190bps to 21.8% on operational leverage
shifts.note: Electrical Solutions Q1 adjusted margin -30bps (commodity/tariff); pricing/productivity offset but tariff inflation is real

shifts.trend: $958M deployed on acquisitions in 2025 to expand product reach in high-growth markets; firm backlog $2.16B at YE25 (+14% from $1.90B)
shifts.note: Backlog substantially all shipping in 2026 — near-term revenue visibility improving

HUBB.verdict: Thesis intact: Hubbell's electrical products are levered to data center construction and T&D upgrade spending — both are multi-year secular demand drivers. The consistent organic growth (7-11%) across consecutive filings confirms the demand is sustained. Tariff inflation is a margin headwind, but pricing power is partially offsetting. Less explosive than the pure-play DC infrastructure names but more durable.

INTC.headline: Supply constraints emerged as a new factor constraining DCAI revenue growth in Q1 2026; Foundry losses stabilized but remain deep at -$2.4B operating loss; NVIDIA $5B investment and 14A strategic pivot are the options

shifts.trend: FY25: Intel 7 supply constraints flagged for Q1 2026 impact; Q1 2026: 'Market demand exceeded available product supply... expect supply constraints to persist at least through H1 2026' — substrate and memory shortages added as new constraints
shifts.note: DCAI volume -5% Q1 despite +22% revenue — premium pricing for limited supply, not a demand failure

shifts.trend: DCAI revenue $14.3B FY25 (+$794M YoY); Q1 2026 $5.1B (+22% YoY) on 27% higher server ASPs from premium mix and demand-based pricing
shifts.note: Intel Foundry external revenue $307M (FY25) → $174M Q1 (Altera now external) — foundry remains effectively internal-only

shifts.trend: FY25 Foundry operating loss $10.3B; Q1 2026 Foundry operating loss $2.4B (vs $2.3B Q1 2025 — not improving); $4.1B Q1 2026 restructuring charges drove $3.7B net loss
shifts.note: NVIDIA $5B equity investment and SoftBank $2B (Sep 2025) provide runway but don't fix foundry economics

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: may pause Intel 14A if no significant external foundry customer; Q1 2026 10-Q: no meaningful update — 14A pause option remains on the table
shifts.note: Intel 18A in high-volume production and ramping — this is the incremental positive; 14A is the strategic question mark

INTC.verdict: Supply constraints are a demand signal — DCAI +22% revenue with -5% volumes shows pricing power under constrained supply. But the foundry is the thesis-breaker risk: $2.4B quarterly operating losses with minimal external revenue mean Intel's manufacturing bet still requires a major external customer win to be viable. The NVIDIA partnership and 18A ramp are the options; the timeline is 2-3 years.

KLAC.headline: Revenue accelerated from +7% to +11% YoY; Korea surged +80% YoY as DRAM/HBM memory investment ramps; China declined to 24% of revenue from 30% as US export controls constrain

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 revenue $3.30B (+7% YoY); Q3 FY26 $3.42B (+11% YoY) — record quarter; Semiconductor Process Control +9% → +13% YoY
shifts.note: Memory (DRAM/HBM) and advanced packaging cited as primary growth drivers in both filings — consistent thesis confirmation

shifts.trend: China $995M (30.2% of total, -9% YoY) in Q2 FY26 → $830M (24% of revenue, +5% YoY) in Q3 FY26; nine-month China revenue flat at $3.09B as US export controls continue constraining
shifts.note: Korea offset: +34% (Q2) → +80% (Q3) on memory investment — geographic rebalancing from China to Korea/Taiwan/North America

shifts.trend: Gross margin 61.4% (Q2 FY26) → 61.1% (Q3 FY26, -0.5pts QoQ) from escalating DRAM chip costs in image computers — framed as transitory by management
shifts.note: 17th consecutive annual dividend increase to $2.30/share/qtr; $10.31B remaining repurchase authorization — capital returns consistent

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact: KLAC is the process control gating input for DRAM/HBM and advanced packaging — exactly the supply chain layers under the most AI-driven demand. Korea +80% and revenue acceleration to +11% are on-thesis signals. China decline to 24% is a known drag but offset by memory geography shift. Gross margin transitory pressure from DRAM input costs is the near-term watch.

LEU.headline: Backlog grew from $3.8B to $3.9B as the $900M HALEU DOE task order and $2.4B contingent LEU contracts anchor long-term demand; JV with Oklo on HALEU deconversion signals ecosystem deepening

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue mix: LEU 77%, Technical Solutions 23%; Q1 FY26 revenue $76.7M (+5%); LEU $44.6M (-13% on -47% SWU volume offset by +52% avg price); Technical Solutions $32.1M (+47%)
shifts.note: Revenue volatility from SWU delivery timing — average price +52% YoY confirms enrichment market remains tight

shifts.trend: SWU spot price $200/unit at Q1 end vs $34 historic 2018 low (+488%); backlog $3.9B at Q1 (vs $3.8B YE25) with $2.4B contingent pending capacity build
shifts.note: Russian supply represents 43% of global enrichment capacity — non-Russian market structurally undersupplied for years

shifts.trend: Dec 2025: began domestic centrifuge manufacturing in Oak Ridge ($560M+ multi-year); Feb 2026: signed Fluor EPC contract for Piketon LEU+HALEU expansion; $62.4M IRS §48C credit allocation
shifts.note: Advanced technology costs jumped to $18.9M Q1 (vs $3.0M, +530%) from Piketon/Oak Ridge expansion — spending inflecting higher

shifts.trend: March 2026: announced exploration of JV with Oklo on HALEU deconversion, co-located at Piketon adjacent to Oklo's planned 1.2 GW Pike County power campus
shifts.note: HALEU deconversion doesn't exist commercially — first-mover advantage if partnership closes

LEU.verdict: Thesis intact: LEU is the only NRC-licensed US HALEU enricher, and the SWU spot market at $200 (vs $34 in 2018) confirms structural tightness. The Oklo JV and Piketon expansion create an integrated nuclear fuel ecosystem. Risk: DOE appropriations risk for future HALEU Operation Contract cascade funding — government budget dependency is significant.

LITE.headline: Revenue accelerated from +65.5% to +90.1% YoY; gross margin expanded 1,130bps → 1,540bps as supply allocation tightened — LITE is the InP/EML laser supply choke point for the 1.6T cycle

shifts.note: OCS (optical circuit switches) contributed >$25M FQ3 (>$38M 9M) — new product category ramping

shifts.trend: Gross margin FQ2 36.1% (+1,130bps YoY) → FQ3 44.2% (+1,540bps YoY); 9M gross margin 38.8% (+1,300bps); operating leverage from supply allocation and 200G-lane mix
shifts.note: Supply allocation driving pricing power — gross margin re-rating is structural, not one-quarter noise

shifts.trend: FQ2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation'; FQ3: language hardened to 'has required us to make decisions on supply allocation' — allocation is deepening
shifts.note: Management investing in manufacturing capacity both internally and with contract manufacturers — supply constraint is a capacity bottleneck, not a demand question

shifts.trend: FQ2: 2 customers at 24%+17% (41% combined); FQ3: 2 customers at 26%+12% (38% combined, top customer rose from 24% to 26%)
shifts.note: Customer consolidation risk — hyperscaler transceiver supply increasingly concentrated

LITE.verdict: Thesis is the strongest in the optics stack: +90% revenue growth with +1,540bps gross margin expansion and hardening supply-allocation language confirms LITE is the InP/EML laser supply choke point. This is a structural re-rating driven by the 1.6T transition, not cycle. The customer concentration (26%+12%) is the only risk — a single hyperscaler demand pause would be impactful.

LRCX.headline: Revenue accelerated from +25% (H1 FY26 YoY) to +24% (nine-month YoY) with DRAM mix jumping from 34% to 39%; China stabilized at 34-35% of revenue across quarters

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 revenue $5.34B (H1 +25% YoY); Q3 FY26 $5.84B (+9% QoQ, nine-month +24% YoY); customer support +6% on growing installed base
shifts.note: Systems revenue driven by DRAM customer investments (+11% QoQ Q3) and customer-support growing consistently on installed-base expansion

shifts.trend: Memory mix: 34% (Q2 FY26) → 39% (Q3 FY26) on strengthened DRAM and NVM investment; Foundry fell from 59% to 54% on lower mature node spend
shifts.note: DRAM/HBM investment is the primary demand driver — same thesis as AMAT, KLAC confirming cross-vendor signal

shifts.trend: China 35% of Q2 FY26 revenue → 34% of Q3 FY26 revenue — stable across quarters after dropping from 43% in Sep 2025
shifts.note: Trade restrictions/tariffs cited as ongoing negative but China stabilizing at 34-35% vs the higher ~40%+ levels from prior years

shifts.trend: Gross margin 49.6% (Q2 FY26) → 49.8% (Q3 FY26, +0.2pts) on better factory efficiencies; deferred revenue $2.25B → dropping as customer down payments normalize
shifts.note: Capital returns strong: Q3 $1.16B repurchases + $326M dividends + $751M debt paydown

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact: LRCX is levered to the DRAM/HBM cycle that is now accelerating (39% of mix), and the installed-base service revenue provides durable baseline. China stabilization removes the overhang of a further sharp decline. Gross margin near 50% is defensible given the installed-base mix. The WFE growth cycle continuing through CY2026 is the core assumption — any memory capex pause would be the key risk.

META.headline: Revenue accelerated from +22% (FY25) to +33% (Q1 2026); capex guidance raised again to $125-145B (from $115-135B); $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments signals infrastructure lock-in

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $201.0B (+22% YoY); Q1 2026 $56.3B (+33% YoY, +29% constant currency); ad impressions FY25 +12%/+9% price; Q1 +19% impressions, +12% price
shifts.note: Revenue acceleration despite Reality Labs $19.2B operating loss (FY25) — advertising engine absorbs massive AI infrastructure spend

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $72.2B; FY26 guidance raised from $115-135B (FY25 10-K) to $125-145B (Q1 2026 10-Q) — $10B guide raise after one quarter
shifts.note: $237.7B non-cancelable commitments at Q1 end; $182.9B in unstarted off-balance-sheet leases (data centers, colo, network) starting 2026-2036

shifts.trend: Operating cash flow $32.2B (Q1 2026) — FY25 debt issuance ($29.9B fixed-rate notes) used to fund capex, not operations; net debt manageable given OCF
shifts.note: RL operating loss $4.03B in Q1 2026; FY26 full-year RL loss expected similar to FY25's $19.2B — ongoing cash burn

META.verdict: Meta is the most aggressive buyer of inference stack infrastructure — raising capex guidance after one quarter by $10B signals demand is outpacing even revised plans. The $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments is perhaps the single largest forward demand signal in this dataset for hardware vendors. Revenue acceleration to +33% shows the AI monetization is already visible. Thesis: META is a structural demand floor for GPUs, power, and cooling.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions accelerated from +24% (Q2 FY26) to +51% (Q3 FY26) YoY; Gentherm Reverse Morris Trust announced to create pure-play data center cooling company

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions Q2 FY26 $454.4M (+24%); Q3 FY26 $544.6M (+51%); data center product sales 9M FY26 up $221.7M YoY; hyperscale/colocation in NA and Europe cited in both filings
shifts.note: Q3 FY26 consolidated net sales $805.0M (+31% total, +24% Climate Solutions revenue run-rate accelerating)

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions operating margin 17.6% (Q1 FY26) → 13.7% (Q2 FY26) → 15.3% (Q3 FY26) — ramp inefficiencies from US data center manufacturing capacity expansion
shifts.note: Margin headwind is explicitly capacity-ramp temporary — $100M incremental capex expansion announced Q2 FY26

shifts.trend: Jan 2026: Reverse Morris Trust announced to spin Performance Technologies, merge with Gentherm — MOD retains Climate Solutions as pure-play DC cooling + commercial HVAC co
shifts.note: Transaction value ~$1.0B; $210M cash to MOD at close; targeted end of CY26 — strategic clarity unlocked

MOD.verdict: Thesis: MOD is reshaping into a pure-play data center cooling company via the Gentherm RMT. Climate Solutions +51% in Q3 FY26 confirms the demand is real. The margin compression is a ramp cost, not a structural problem. Post-close, investors get a focused pure-play story — the strategic transformation is on-thesis and the revenue trajectory supports the execution.

MRVL.headline: Data center revenue +46% YoY (FY26) with gross margin expanded +9.7pts; Celestial AI (photonic fabric) and XConn (CXL/PCIe) acquisitions extend the custom AI ASIC roadmap to silicon photonics

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 (Dec qtr) DC revenue $1.52B (+38% YoY); FY26 full year DC $6.1B (+46% YoY, 74% of mix); total FY26 revenue $8.2B (+42% YoY)
shifts.note: FY26 full-year data center growth (+46%) accelerated from the +38% Q3 pace — sequential ramp through the year

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 gross margin 51.6% (vs 23.0% prior year Q3, which included $356.8M restructuring impairments); FY26 gross margin 51.0% vs 41.3% prior year (+9.7pts)
shifts.note: Prior-year comparisons inflated by one-time charges — underlying normalized margin improvement is ~5pts

shifts.trend: Post-FY26: Celestial AI closed Feb 2, 2026 (~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares) for Photonic Fabric scale-up interconnect; XConn closed Feb 10, 2026 ($280M + 2.1M shares) for PCIe/CXL switching
shifts.note: 5nm custom ASIC in production, 3nm in design, 2nm/1.4nm in development — roadmap explicit on silicon photonics and custom HBM integration

shifts.trend: Direct Customer A = 13-14% of FY26 revenue; Distributor A = 34-37% — combined ~51% from two channels; top 10 customers ~81% of revenue historically
shifts.note: Distributor A acts as a hyperscaler conduit — high concentration is inherent in custom ASIC business model

MRVL.verdict: Thesis is the custom ASIC and photonic interconnect stack: data center +46% with expanding gross margins, and Celestial AI adding photonic fabric puts MRVL at the convergence of custom silicon and silicon photonics. The concentration (one distributor at 37%) is the XPU hyperscaler bet — aligned with thesis but creates asymmetric downside on any hyperscaler design change.

MSFT.headline: Azure accelerated from +39% (Q2 FY26) to +40% (Q3 FY26) YoY; commercial RPO maintained near $625-627B; quarterly capex $29.9B → $30.9B with no sign of deceleration

shifts.trend: Azure +39% (Q2 FY26) → +40% (Q3 FY26) YoY; Microsoft Cloud +26% → +29%; total revenue +17% → +18%
shifts.note: 9-month MS Cloud revenue $155.1B (+27%) — sustained acceleration across the product portfolio

shifts.trend: Q2 capex $29.9B (+89% YoY); Q3 capex $30.9B; 9M total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year — consistent $30B quarterly run-rate in place
shifts.note: H1 capex $49.3B vs $30.7B prior year; commercial RPO $625B (Q2) → $627B (Q3) — demand commitments stable at ~$625B level

shifts.trend: Microsoft Cloud gross margin dropped to 67% (Q2 FY26) on AI infrastructure investments; Q3 operating income +20% suggesting SG&A leverage partially offsets
shifts.note: $5.9B 9M non-operating gain from OpenAI Recapitalization dilution (public benefit corp conversion) — one-time positive

MSFT.verdict: Azure is the most consistent AI demand engine across the dataset — two consecutive quarters of 39-40% growth with a $30B quarterly capex run-rate and ~$625B commercial RPO. The cloud gross margin compression (67%) is the trade-off for infrastructure investment. Thesis: MSFT's capex trajectory is the single most durable signal for the entire compute stack — every layer depends on it continuing.

MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ with ~81% gross margin

shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B (Q1) → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+); DRAM ASPs +mid-110% YoY Q2
shifts.note: Q2 beat: $23.86B actual vs $18.7B guide (+28%); EPS $12.20 vs $8.42 guide (+45%)

shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% (Q1) → 74.9% (Q2) → ~81% guide (Q3) — approximately +24pts of expansion across two quarters
shifts.note: CMBU (HBM/hyperscale) operating margin 55% (Q1) → 66% (Q2) — pricing power compounding with supply scarcity

shifts.trend: Q2 guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided $33.5B (+40% QoQ implied)
shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on Q3 guide is high

shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language across two consecutive filings
shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters — tightness is persistent

shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30% hike); $7.49B of $10B buyback already executed
shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; signals management cycle durability conviction

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026. The 30% dividend hike is management's confidence signal. Main risk: HBM single-customer concentration (one customer at 13% of six-month revenue).

MXL.headline: Revenue recovery accelerated from +30% (FY25) to +43% (Q1 2026) YoY; Infrastructure optical DSP growth confirmed as primary driver; customer concentration improved with top customer falling from >20% to 13%

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $467.6M (+30% YoY recovery); Q1 2026 $137.2M (+43% YoY); infrastructure optical/wireless backhaul the growth driver in both periods
shifts.note: FY25: Broadband +$87.6M (+59%); Q1 2026: Infrastructure +$36.3M led by optical/wireless backhaul/access — different segments sequentially driving recovery

shifts.trend: FY25 top-10 customers = 65% of revenue, top customer historically >20%; Q1 2026 top customer 13%, top-10 = 56% — base broadening
shifts.note: Customer concentration easing is a positive for thesis durability — less single-customer dependency

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 57% (+3pts YoY) on mix and lower intangible amortization; Q1 2026 volume-led growth with no material ASP changes — margin trajectory positive
shifts.note: R&D -7% FY25 on workforce reductions; mgmt flagged R&D needs to re-expand for next-gen (1.6T) products

MXL.verdict: MXL is an early-cycle optical DSP recovery story — the 400G to 800G to 1.6T switch interconnect transition is the next-leg opportunity. Revenue acceleration to +43% confirms the recovery is sustained. Customer concentration improvement is a positive structural change. The risk is that MXL is early-stage in the 1.6T ramp and R&D re-investment needs to accelerate — execution on next-gen product timing is the key risk.

NOW.headline: Revenue consistent at +21-22% YoY across both periods; RPO steady at $27.7-28.2B (+25% YoY); Now Assist AI agent adoption deepening across ITSM/CSM/HRSD with no consumption disappointment visible yet

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $13.28B (+21% YoY); Q1 2026 $3.77B (+22% YoY); subscription revenue +21-22% consistently; RPO +27% (FY25) → +25% (Q1 2026)
shifts.note: Self-hosted upfront (US federal) $492M FY25; Q1 2026 $138M (vs $157M Q1 2025) — slight decline suggests mix normalizing

shifts.trend: FY26 guidance: subscription revenue to increase in absolute dollars, ~flat as % of total; consistent across both filings
shifts.note: No beat-and-raise cadence — ServiceNow guides conservatively and meets; now Assist agent pricing is consumption-based (uncertainty acknowledged)

shifts.trend: Subscription gross margin slightly pressured by self-hosted mix (Q1 2026); overall margins broadly stable
shifts.note: OBBBA July 2025 enacted — immediate domestic R&D expensing is a US tax benefit

NOW.verdict: Thesis intact: NOW is a steady 20-22% compounder with RPO growth supporting visibility. The Now Assist AI agent adoption is the key incremental driver — if consumption pricing materializes at scale, the +25% RPO growth understates future revenue. Risk: RPO growth (25%) is decelerating slightly from 27%, and self-hosted upfront declining Q1 — watch whether enterprise AI agent adoption converts consumption commitments.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66-68% YoY; four-way customer concentration newly disclosed at >10% each; China effectively zero after $4.5B H20 inventory charge; supply/energy constraint language unchanged

shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp; Q1 FY27 guided $43.0B at 73.5% non-GAAP GM
shifts.note: Blackwell is now the majority of DC revenue; Rubin on annual cadence for next year

shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22%, 15%, 13%, 11%); FY26 10-K did not change this disclosure — hyperscaler dependence is structurally embedded
shifts.note: Four customers represent 61% of revenue — any one of them pausing deployments is a material event

shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
shifts.note: China optionality is fully impaired — model at zero going forward

shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3 FY26) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within the 73-74% target range
shifts.note: Q3 margin down 1.2pts YoY; Q1 FY27 guide holds — Blackwell cost structure improving as it ramps

shifts.trend: Both filings cite data center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint — quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.note: $17.5B in private/infra investments + $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees; $7.5B new DC leases FY26 Q4-FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. New disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis. China optionality fully impaired and should be modeled at zero. Q1 FY27 $43B guidance and 73.5% GM confirm the cycle hasn't peaked.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection (enclosures + liquid cooling) +42% FY25 → +76% Q1 FY26 organic; segment margin expanded to 22.7% as data center infrastructure demand compounds

shifts.trend: FY25 net sales $3.89B (+30%); Systems Protection +42.2% to $2.59B; Q1 FY26 net sales $1.24B (+53.5%); Systems Protection +76.1% to $895M (organic +50.1%)
shifts.note: Infrastructure vertical (incl. data centers) drove ~46pts of Systems Protection organic growth in Q1 FY26; Avail EPG acquisition contributed $137.7M

shifts.trend: FY25 Systems Protection segment margin 20.7% (-1.4pts from capacity investment + tariffs); Q1 FY26 segment income +95%, margin expanded 220bps to 22.7% on volume leverage
shifts.note: Consolidated gross margin 37.7% (FY25) → 35.9% (Q1 FY26, -290bps) on tariffs/raw mat'l — segment-level improving but blended declining

shifts.trend: Closed $1.0B Avail Infrastructure Solutions (EPG) May 2025; backlog $2.3B at YE25; Q1 FY26 backlog growing on data center demand
shifts.note: Systems Protection organic growth ~17% in FY25 with data centers driving ~16pts — near-pure-play on data center enclosures and liquid cooling

NVT.verdict: Thesis: nVent is a liquid cooling and enclosure pure-play accelerating into the AI data center cycle — Systems Protection +76% organic in Q1 FY26 is exceptional for an industrial. The segment margin expansion to 22.7% despite tariff headwinds confirms pricing power. The Avail EPG acquisition is adding scale. Main risk: consolidated gross margin -290bps from tariffs/raw materials is a watch point.

OKLO.headline: Order book grew ~2,500% since 2023 SPAC; Meta prepayment agreement for 1.2 GW adds the first hyperscaler contracted revenue; first commercial Aurora deployment targets 2028

shifts.trend: Order book ~18,100 MWe (Q3 FY25) incl. 12 GW Switch MPA + 4,750 MWe new non-binding LOIs; Meta prepayment agreement (FY25 10-K) for 1.2 GW in Pike County, Ohio
shifts.note: First hyperscaler prepayment for advanced fission — Meta agreement is the de-risking catalyst for commercial viability

shifts.trend: 9M FY25 cash used in operations $48.7M; FY25 opex $139.3M (+164% YoY); accumulated deficit $199.3M; FY26 guidance: $80-100M op cash burn + $350-450M capex
shifts.note: Raised $968M in equity financing through FY25; Dec 2025 $300M ATM at $88.29 avg — ongoing dilution required

shifts.trend: NRC Phase I pre-application assessment completed with no significant gaps (Jul 2025); DOE Aurora Safety Design Agreement approved Feb 2026; first commercial deployment target 2028 maintained
shifts.note: 3 of 11 DOE Reactor Pilot Program projects awarded (Aug 2025) — regulatory progress is a real milestone

OKLO.verdict: Pre-revenue, pre-deployment story — the Meta prepayment agreement is the single most important de-risking catalyst, confirming hyperscaler willingness to fund advanced fission. The 2028 target is the key milestone. The thesis is intact as a long-duration option on nuclear microreactors for AI power, but the burn rate ($80-100M/yr ops + $350-450M capex) requires continued equity access. Execution risk is high.

ORCL.headline: OCI revenue accelerated from +68% (Q2 FY26) to +84% (Q3 FY26) YoY; RPO sustained at $523-553B as cloud contract signings continue; 9-month capex $39.2B (3x prior year)

shifts.trend: Cloud infrastructure revenue Q2 $4.08B (+68% YoY) → Q3 $4.89B (+84% YoY); total revenue Q2 $16.06B (+14%) → Q3 $17.19B (+22%)
shifts.note: OCI is the pure AI-workload signal — +84% constant-currency growth is the fastest in the hyperscaler peer set for this period

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 capex $20.5B vs $6.3B H1 FY25 (3.3x); 9M FY26 capex $39.2B vs $12.1B 9M FY25 (3.2x); trend expected 'next few fiscal years'
shifts.note: Issued $42.7B senior notes (Sep 2025 + Feb 2026) + $5B Mandatory Convertible Preferred + ATM program — debt-financed build-out at scale

shifts.trend: RPO $523.3B (Nov 30 2025) → $552.6B (Feb 28 2026); Q2 was 5.4x YoY leap from $97.3B; large cloud contracts signed in both periods sustaining the RPO level
shifts.note: 9M operating margin declining as infrastructure spend ramps — margin pressure is a temporary investment signal

ORCL.verdict: OCI is the highest-growth cloud infrastructure platform in the dataset (+84% Q3 vs AWS +28%, Azure +40%, Google Cloud +63%). The RPO-to-capex model — commit cloud capacity to customers, then build to deliver — is the demand validation. Risk: $42.7B debt issuance in six months creates a highly leveraged balance sheet; margin pressure will persist until infrastructure achieves scale.

PLTR.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +53% (FY25) to +85% (Q1 2026) YoY; commercial re-accelerated to +95% — first time commercial outpaced government; operating margin expanded from 31% to 46%

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $4.5B (+53% YoY, government $2.40B +53%, commercial $2.07B +60%); Q1 2026 $1.63B (+85% YoY, government $858M +76%, commercial $774M +95%)
shifts.note: Revenue growth acceleration from +53% to +85% with commercial outpacing government (+95% vs +76%) — AI Platform commercialization taking hold

shifts.trend: FY25 income from operations $1.41B (31% of revenue) vs $310M prior year (4.5x growth); Q1 2026 operating income $754M (46% margin) vs $176M (20%) prior year
shifts.note: Gross margin 87% (Q1 2026, vs 80% Q1 2025); contribution margins both segments 73% (vs 61%) — operating leverage substantial

shifts.trend: FY25: 74% US revenue, 26% international; Q1 2026: US $1.28B (79% of total, vs 71% in Q1 2025); no customer >10% Q1 2026 (vs some concentration prior)
shifts.note: US concentration increasing (79% of revenue) but no single-customer risk; government 54% / commercial 46% mix stable

PLTR.verdict: Thesis is the strongest across the software names: +85% revenue growth and 46% operating margin in Q1 2026 are exceptional metrics. Commercial overtaking government growth at +95% confirms the AI platform is commercializing broadly. The 87% gross margin leaves enormous leverage to scale. Main risk: high valuation multiples embed significant growth continuity — any deceleration would be sharply punished.

PWR.headline: Backlog reached $48.47B (+10% QoQ from $43.98B); RPO $26.24B (+10% QoQ); Q1 revenue +26% YoY with operating income +42% — data center is the primary growth catalyst

shifts.trend: FY25 backlog $43.98B (+27.3% YoY), RPO $23.76B (+41.8%); Q1 FY26 backlog $48.47B (+10.2% QoQ), RPO $26.24B (+10.4% QoQ) — continuous sequential backlog growth
shifts.note: Both filings cite data center as primary growth catalyst; 'strong demand will continue' language in Q1 FY26

shifts.trend: FY25 revenues $28.5B (+20.3%); Q1 FY26 $7.87B (+26.3% YoY) — growth holding and slightly accelerating
shifts.note: Electric segment +$1.52B in Q1 FY26; Underground +$117M; DSI and CEI acquisitions contributing to DC infrastructure exposure

shifts.trend: FY25 Electric segment margin 10.3% (flat); Q1 FY26 operating margin 4.3% (vs 3.8%), gross margin 14.1% (vs 13.4%); net income +55%
shifts.note: Turnkey DC infrastructure (CEI + DSI acquisitions) driving higher margin work in the Underground segment

PWR.verdict: Thesis intact: Quanta's backlog is a real-time demand meter for US grid and data center infrastructure spending — $48.47B and growing, with double-digit sequential increases. Operating margin expansion (+50bps Q1 FY26) confirms the mix shift to higher-value DC turnkey work is accruing margin. The DSI acquisition for mechanical/plumbing/process infrastructure extends the addressable DC opportunity.

SNOW.headline: Revenue maintained +29-31% YoY across both periods; RPO grew from $7.9B to $9.8B (+24%); $1M+ customers grew +27% to 733 — enterprise AI/data workload adoption scaling up

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 product revenue $1.16B (+29% YoY); FY26 full year $4.7B (+31% YoY vs FY25 $3.6B); consistent 29-31% growth rate maintained
shifts.note: Customers >$1M trailing revenue 576 (FY25) → 733 (FY26 +27%) — enterprise penetration deepening

shifts.trend: Forbes Global 2000: 790 customers contributing ~43% of revenue; NRR 125% (vs 126% Q3 FY26, vs 127% FY25) — modest moderation from peak but still strong
shifts.note: Consumption model means RPO is not a direct revenue predictor; 46% of RPO expected in next 12 months ($4.5B forward coverage)

shifts.trend: Net loss $1.3B (FY26); SBC ~34% of revenue (down from 41%) — cash efficiency improving; operating cash flow $1.2B for FY26
shifts.note: Began ASC 985-20 self-hosted accounting for public sector in Q1 FY26 — revenue recognition change for government contracts

SNOW.verdict: Thesis: SNOW is the AI data layer enabling consumption of structured enterprise data for AI/ML workloads — NRR of 125% and $1M+ customer growth +27% show the enterprise is expanding usage. The RPO growth ($7.9B → $9.8B) is the future revenue signal. Main risk: NRR moderation (127% → 125%) and the consumption-based model mean revenue can miss if AI workloads don't consume at contracted rates. Not a supply-chain play — more a demand multiplier.

SNPS.headline: Revenue +15% (FY25) then +66% (Q1 FY26 on Ansys consolidation); Design Automation +96% on Ansys vs Design IP -6%; $13.5B debt being aggressively paid down before buyback resumes

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $7.05B (+15% YoY, $756.6M from Ansys 3.5 months); Q1 FY26 $2.41B (+66% YoY, $885.6M from Ansys full quarter); organic EDA +~19% ex-Ansys
shifts.note: China revenue -22% ex-Ansys in FY25 on BIS restrictions; Q1 FY26 China 'challenging near-term' reiterated

shifts.trend: FY25-end debt $13.5B ($10B Senior Notes + $4.3B term loan); Q1 FY26: paid off $3.5B term loan fully; aggressive debt reduction before share repurchase resumes
shifts.note: Operating income compressed by $394.1M Ansys intangible amortization (Q1 FY26) and $118.3M restructuring charge — reported earnings understate underlying economics

shifts.trend: Design Automation +96% Q1 FY26 on Ansys; Design IP -6% YoY — structural divergence within SNPS: EDA/simulation strong, IP weak
shifts.note: Jan 2026: agreed to divest Processor IP business to GlobalFoundries — portfolio reallocation toward highest-growth EDA/simulation

shifts.trend: FY25 China revenue -22% ex-Ansys (BIS restrictions); Q1 FY26 guidance reiterated challenging China; Design IP further hurt by BIS 'is-informed' letter May 2025 (rescinded Jul 2025 but uncertainty lingered)
shifts.note: SNPS China exposure worse than CDNS — Design IP weakness from BIS plus foundry customer softness

SNPS.verdict: The Ansys integration is the dominant story — it transformed SNPS from EDA-only to EDA+simulation, making it the broadest chip-design software platform. Debt paydown trajectory and Design IP divestiture are the right moves. China headwinds and Design IP weakness are near-term overhangs. The thesis for infrastructure stack exposure (every advanced chip uses SNPS to design and simulate) is intact.

VRT.headline: Revenue grew from +27.7% (FY25) to +30.1% (Q1 FY26); Americas +53% in Q1 with 27.0% operating margin; backlog more than doubled to $15.0B (YE25) and continues building

shifts.note: Americas re-acceleration to +53% in Q1 vs +42% FY25 — AI/HPC demand compounding sequentially

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $7.2B (YE24) → $15.0B (YE25, more than doubled); majority firm and expected to ship in next 12-18 months; 'very robust' demand language in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Backlog doubling in one year is the strongest supply/demand signal in the cooling/power infrastructure peer set

shifts.trend: Americas operating margin 24.4% (FY25) → 27.0% (Q1 FY26); consolidated gross margin 36.3% (FY25) → 37.7% (Q1 FY26, +400bps YoY) on mix and volume leverage
shifts.note: SG&A 15.8% of sales (-130bps FY25); net income Q1 +137% YoY on operating leverage and interest income vs expense swing

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M; FY26 guided $425-525M (reaffirmed Q1 FY26) — 2x+ increase for capacity expansion across global manufacturing footprint
shifts.note: Hybrid air-and-liquid + HVDC architectures cited as next-gen product drivers; AI/HPC demand 'very robust'

VRT.verdict: Thesis is one of the most compelling in the dataset: backlog doubling, Americas margin at 27%, gross margin +400bps — all simultaneously. VRT is the pure-play data center power and cooling infrastructure provider and demand is compounding. The $425-525M capex raise doubles the investment in manufacturing capacity. Main risk: if AI buildout slows or commodity inflation exceeds pricing, the margin expansion reverses — but current signals all point the other direction.
